evidence: Runtime composition is dominated by large callback bags and manual merge/validate plumbing across modules: `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` (19 callback fields) plus `withDefaults` and `callback` switch in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, `importerRuntime` (25+ callback fields) plus `ensureForWorkflow`/`withDefaultsImporterRuntime` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, and many `Runtime*Ops` structs in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`.

Several exported APIs are thin pass-through wrappers adding little policy/value, e.g. `ConnectToWifi`/`DisconnectWifi`/`ToggleDevice`/`ListWifiSSIDs` in `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` and `Publish*`/`*Transitions` wrapper pairs in `goseg/docker/events/events.go`.

Wide embedded config bags (`SysConfig`, `UrbitDocker`) create cross-domain coupling and repetitive subconfig adapter boilerplate (`persistShip*Config` family and `persistUrbitSubconfigWith` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`; large patch registry in `goseg/config/config_update_registry.go`).

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtimes add heavy indirection cost without proportional leverage
evidence: `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` and `startupInitRuntime` each rely on many function fields plus manual `withDefaults` and key-based callback switches.
`importerRuntime` declares many injected callbacks and then duplicates nil-guard logic in `ensureForWorkflow` and assignment logic in `withDefaultsImporterRuntime`.
`RuntimeUrbitConfigOps` and related `Runtime*Ops` structs in orchestration expose many function slots that are mostly forwarding existing package functions.
findings.suggestion: Replace callback bags with smaller capability interfaces per workflow (startup init, background services, import workflow, urbit config persistence) and use typed constructors that require mandatory deps at compile time; keep optional behavior behind explicit options structs instead of dozens of nullable function fields.

findings.summary: Multiple wrapper APIs are mostly pass-through and duplicate surface area
evidence: WiFi API functions repeatedly do `resolve runtime` then one method call (`ListWifiSSIDs`, `ConnectToWifi`, `DisconnectWifi`, `ToggleDevice`, `applyCaptiveRules`) with minimal added behavior.
Event API has paired wrappers (`Publish*` and `*Transitions`) that mostly resolve broker/default context and forward to broker methods.
C2C entrypoints (`C2CMode`, `UnaliveC2C`, `C2CConnect`) always allocate a new flow and immediately forward to a single method.
findings.suggestion: Collapse pass-through layers by exposing a cohesive service object (or package-level minimal API) per domain with explicit lifecycle; keep only wrappers that enforce policy, translation, or compatibility and remove redundant aliases.

findings.summary: Embedded mega-config models leak across workflows and force repetitive adapter code
evidence: `SysConfig` embeds multiple domains (connectivity, runtime, auth session, startram, penpai), and `UrbitDocker` embeds six concern structs.

Ship workflow persistence repeats near-identical per-subconfig functions (`persistShipRuntimeConfig`, `persistShipNetworkConfig`, `persistShipFeatureConfig`, `persistShipWebConfig`, `persistShipScheduleConfig`, `persistShipBackupConfig`) that differ mainly by getter/setter glue.
Config update registry centralizes many field-level patch adapters on the broad `SysConfig`, reinforcing a single shared mutable bag instead of narrower bounded contexts.
findings.suggestion: Introduce bounded config ports per domain (connectivity/runtime/startram/penpai and per-urbit subdomains) and move callsites to domain-specific mutation APIs; keep compatibility mapping at boundaries only, then retire duplicated subconfig adapter functions.
